A gating module dynamically selects relevant memory blocks, which are then iteratively processed by a reasoning module with an evolving working memory to solve downstream tasks. The compressor and reasoner are jointly optimized via end-to-end reinforcement learning, while the gating module is trained separately as a classifier. Experimental results show that the proposed method achieves competitive accuracy on multi-hop reasoning benchmarks such as RULER-HQA, extrapolates context length from 7K to 1.75M tokens, and offers a favorable accuracy-efficiency trade-off compared to strong long-context baselines. In particular, it achieves up to a 2 times reduction in peak GPU memory usage and a 6 times inference speedup over MemAgent.

keyInsights: Cognitively inspired architectures that separate compressed long-term memory from dynamic working memory can effectively scale LLMs to ultra-long contexts.
Dynamic and state-dependent memory recall, conditioned on the evolving reasoning state, is crucial for accurate multi-hop reasoning over compressed information, outperforming static retrieval.

Jointly optimizing memory compression and the reasoning process via end-to-end reinforcement learning significantly enhances the quality of compressed representations for downstream tasks, yielding optimal performance.
originalProblem: Large Language Models (LLMs) face quadratic computational costs and information forgetting when processing ultra-long contexts, limiting scalability and practical utility.
Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) systems suffer from context fragmentation, hindering multi-hop and implicit semantic reasoning across disparate text chunks.
Existing solutions like sparse attention often trade efficiency for accuracy, while recurrent memory-augmented LLMs are too slow for very long sequences due to serial processing.
results: LycheeMemory successfully extrapolates context length to 1.75 million tokens, demonstrating superior scalability compared to existing methods.
It achieves a 6x inference speedup compared to MemAgent and a 2x reduction in peak GPU memory usage, exhibiting a near-constant inference latency curve with increasing context length.
The framework maintains competitive sub-EM accuracy on multi-hop reasoning benchmarks and shows robust zero-shot generalization across diverse tasks like unseen multi-document QA and summarization.
solution: A dual-system framework, LycheeMemory, is proposed, inspired by human memory's separation of compressed long-term memory and dynamic working memory.
A Compressor component transforms raw text chunks into compact, high-fidelity KV-cache representations, forming an efficient compressed memory bank.

A dynamic Gate module performs state-dependent retrieval, selectively recalling relevant compressed memory blocks based on the current query and evolving working memory for iterative processing by a Reasoner component.

An end-to-end reinforcement learning strategy jointly optimizes the Compressor and Reasoner to learn 'reasoning-aware' memory representations, ensuring alignment between compression and downstream reasoning.
paper_summary.summary: A dual-system framework called LycheeMemory enables large language models to process and reason over ultra-long contexts, up to 1.75 million tokens, by combining compressed memory representations with dynamic, state-dependent recall and iterative reasoning. This approach delivers a 6x inference speedup and 2x reduction in peak GPU memory while maintaining competitive multi-hop reasoning accuracy.

title: Dynamic Long Context Reasoning over Compressed Memory via End-to-End Reinforcement Learning

abstract: The scalability of large language models for long-context reasoning is severely constrained by the linear growth of their Transformer key-value cache, which incurs significant memory and computational costs. We posit that as a model generates reasoning tokens, the informational value of past generated tokens diminishes, creating an opportunity for compression. In this work, we propose to periodically compress the generation KV cache with a learned, special-purpose token and evict compressed entries. We train the model to perform this compression via a modified joint distillation and reinforcement learning (RL) framework. Our training method minimizes overhead over the conventional RL process, as it leverages RL outputs for distillation. Empirically, our method achieves a superior memory-accuracy Pareto frontier compared to both the model without cache compression and training-free compression techniques.
title: Breadcrumbs Reasoning: Memory-Efficient Reasoning with Compression Beacons

abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) exhibit enhanced capabilities by Chain-of-Thought reasoning. However, the extended reasoning sequences introduce significant GPU memory overhead due to increased key-value (KV) cache. Existing KV cache compression methods mitigate memory bottlenecks but struggle in long reasoning tasks. In this paper, we analyze attention patterns in reasoning tasks and reveal a Token Importance Recurrence phenomenon: a large proportion of tokens regain high attention after multiple decoding steps, which is failed to capture by existing works and may lead to unpredictable eviction on such periodically critical tokens. To address this, we propose LazyEviction, an observation window-based lagged eviction framework retaining latent recurring tokens by prioritized eviction based on tokens' recurrence patterns. Extensive experiments demonstrate that LazyEviction reduces KV cache by 50%~70% while maintaining comparable accuracy, outperforming existing KV cache compression baselines. Our implementation code can be found at this https URL.

keyInsights: The Token Importance Recurrence (TIR) phenomenon demonstrates that tokens temporarily exhibiting low attention frequently regain high importance across multiple decoding steps in long reasoning sequences.

Traditional greedy, per-step KV cache eviction strategies are suboptimal for long reasoning as they fail to anticipate and preserve these recurring critical tokens, leading to a loss of "knowledge continuity."
A predictive retention strategy, which observes attention patterns over an interval and leverages recurrence metrics, can more effectively manage KV cache and maintain reasoning accuracy.
originalProblem: Large Language Models (LLMs) incur high Key-Value (KV) cache memory overhead during long reasoning tasks, limiting their efficiency and length capacity.
Existing greedy KV cache compression methods prematurely evict tokens that may become important later, leading to accuracy degradation in multi-step reasoning.
The bottleneck of KV cache memory often exceeds GPU capacity for very long sequences, necessitating more effective memory management without compromising reasoning quality.
results: LazyEviction consistently outperformed all tested state-of-the-art KV cache compression methods across diverse reasoning benchmarks, including mathematical (GSM8K, MATH-500, AIME), scientific (GPQA), and programming (LiveCodeBench) tasks.
The method achieved a 50%-70% reduction in KV cache memory while maintaining accuracy comparable to, and sometimes exceeding, that of an uncompressed FullKV cache.

For very long reasoning sequences (e.g., 16K tokens), LazyEviction demonstrated improved overall inference efficiency (throughput and average latency) compared to FullKV, showcasing its practical benefits.
solution: LazyEviction implements an Observation Window-based Lagged Eviction Mechanism, deferring eviction decisions to periodic intervals instead of making them at every decoding step.
It introduces Recurrence Interval Tracking (MRI) for each token, which records the longest observed period between consecutive activations to quantify temporal importance.

An MRI-Centric Eviction Policy calculates an importance score based on a token's MRI and time since last activation, guiding the retention of critical recurring tokens while evicting less important ones.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers from the Hong Kong University of Science and Technology and the Hong Kong Polytechnic University developed LazyEviction, a KV cache management method that observes attention patterns over time. This approach achieved a 50%-70% KV cache memory reduction and consistently outperformed existing methods in long reasoning tasks across various LLMs, often matching or exceeding the accuracy of an uncompressed cache.

title: LazyEviction: Lagged KV Eviction with Attention Pattern Observation for Efficient Long Reasoning

abstract: The reasoning capabilities of large language models (LLMs) have improved substantially through increased test-time computation, typically in the form of intermediate tokens known as chain-of-thought (CoT). However, CoT often becomes unnecessarily long, increasing computation cost without actual accuracy gains or sometimes even degrading performance, a phenomenon known as ``overthinking''. We propose a multi-stage efficient reasoning method that combines supervised fine-tuning -- via rejection sampling or reasoning trace reformatting -- with reinforcement learning using an adaptive length penalty. We introduce a lightweight reward function that penalizes tokens generated after the first correct answer but encouraging self-verification only when beneficial. We conduct a holistic evaluation across seven diverse reasoning tasks, analyzing the accuracy--response length trade-off. Our approach reduces response length by an average of 28\% for 8B models and 40\% for 32B models, while incurring only minor performance drops of 1.6 and 2.5 points, respectively. Despite its conceptual simplicity, it achieves a superior trade-off compared to more complex state-of-the-art efficient reasoning methods, scoring 76.6, in terms of the area under the Overthinking-Adjusted Accuracy curve ($\text{AUC}_{\text{OAA}}$) -- 5 points above the base model and 2.5 points above the second-best approach.

keyInsights: Penalizing tokens only after the first correct answer promotes conciseness while still allowing for necessary self-correction and exploration within the reasoning trace.
Supervised Fine-Tuning serves as a crucial warm-up stage, significantly improving RL convergence and the overall efficiency-accuracy trade-off.
The "overthinking" behavior in LLMs is a general phenomenon, and the proposed adaptive reasoning solution generalizes across diverse tasks despite being trained exclusively on math problems.
originalProblem: Large Language Models (LLMs) frequently exhibit "overthinking" behavior in Chain-of-Thought (CoT) reasoning, generating excessively long, repetitive, or even detrimental traces.
This verbosity leads to increased inference costs, slower response times, and can sometimes paradoxically degrade model accuracy.
Existing methods for efficient reasoning are often non-adaptive, relying on rigid length constraints that may truncate essential steps or allow for too much verbosity.
results: The methods achieved a superior accuracy-response length trade-off, with Format-Adaptive-Answer showing an average AUC_OAA of 76.6, a 5-point improvement over the base model.
For the Qwen3-8B model, response length was reduced by 28-33% with only a 1.2-1.6 point accuracy drop, while for Qwen3-32B, the reduction reached 40%.
The approach proved scalable and effective across various model sizes (1.7B, 8B, 32B), with the efficiency gains becoming more pronounced for larger LLMs.
solution: A multi-stage training framework combining Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT) with Reinforcement Learning (RL) is proposed to induce adaptive and efficient reasoning.
The SFT stage employs either rejection sampling (selecting the shortest correct responses) or trace reformatting (removing redundant summaries) to bias the model towards conciseness.

The RL stage uses Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) with a novel adaptive length penalty that applies only to tokens generated *after* the first correct answer is produced, discouraging post-solution overthinking.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers from Universitat Pompeu Fabra and Amazon AI Labs developed a multi-stage training framework that reduces Large Language Model (LLM) response length on reasoning tasks while preserving accuracy. The approach decreased the average response length for Qwen3-8B by 28-33% with minimal accuracy loss (1.2-1.6 points) and showed a 40% reduction for Qwen3-32B, improving overall efficiency and the accuracy-response length trade-off.

title: Correct, Concise and Complete: Multi-stage Training For Adaptive Reasoning

abstract: Large reasoning models (LRMs) achieve strong performance through extended reasoning traces, but they often exhibit overthinking behavior for low-complexity queries. Existing efforts to mitigate this issue are fundamentally limited by unstable accuracy-efficiency trade-offs and poor robustness to heterogeneous reasoning behaviors. To address these challenges, we propose a two-stage framework for stable adaptive thinking in LRMs. The framework first applies Hybrid Fine-Tuning to expose the model to both thinking and no-thinking behaviors, establishing well-conditioned initialization. It then performs adaptive reinforcement learning with Correctness-Preserving Advantage Shaping (CPAS) to avoid suppressing correct long-chain reasoning, and Length-Aware Gradient Regulation (LAGR) to stabilize optimization under severe reasoning-length heterogeneity. Extensive experiments on Qwen2.5-1.5B and 7B show consistent improvements over strong baselines, achieving up to +3.7/+3.6 accuracy points while reducing generated tokens by 40.6%/43.9%. Further analyses across varying problem difficulties and out-of-distribution tasks confirm the robustness and generalization of our approach.

keyInsights: Correctness-Preserving Advantage Shaping (CPAS) is crucial for stable RL training, preventing mode collapse by ensuring correct long-chain reasoning is not suppressed due to length penalties.

Length-Aware Gradient Regulation (LAGR) effectively mitigates training instability and bias caused by highly heterogeneous reasoning lengths by intelligently rebalancing gradient contributions and boosting decision signals.

A two-stage approach, starting with supervised Hybrid Fine-Tuning before RL, provides a robust initialization for the model to learn diverse reasoning behaviors, simplifying subsequent adaptive decision-making.
originalProblem: Large Reasoning Models (LRMs) suffer from "overthinking," generating unnecessarily long reasoning chains for simple queries, leading to high inference costs and latency.

Existing adaptive thinking methods either restrict reasoning capacity, require complex multi-model setups, or face training instability due to the accuracy-efficiency trade-off and heterogeneous reasoning lengths.
Reinforcement learning (RL) based adaptive thinking struggles with mode collapse and imbalanced gradient contributions, making stable optimization difficult.
results: The method improved accuracy by up to 3.7 points on mathematical benchmarks (e.g., AIME) while reducing generated tokens by up to 43.9% for Qwen2.5-7B models, demonstrating a superior accuracy-efficiency trade-off.
The model exhibited difficulty-aware adaptability, using the "no-thinking" mode for 77.6% of easier MATH-500 queries and increasing "thinking" mode usage for more challenging problems like AIME.

It generalized robustly to out-of-distribution tasks such as GPQA, achieving the highest accuracy (+2.9 points over baseline) and a 51.0% reduction in average response length, while adaptively choosing reasoning modes.
solution: A two-stage framework, Stable Adaptive Thinking, first uses Hybrid Fine-Tuning (HFT) to warm up the model with both thinking and no-thinking behaviors.
The second stage employs Reinforcement Learning (RL) with two novel mechanisms: Correctness-Preserving Advantage Shaping (CPAS) and Length-Aware Gradient Regulation (LAGR).

CPAS modifies advantage signals to prevent penalizing correct long-chain reasoning, while LAGR rebalances gradient contributions and boosts the control token's signal to stabilize optimization under length heterogeneity.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers from Beihang University, Shanghai AI Laboratory, and other institutions developed Stable Adaptive Thinking, a two-stage framework that enables Large Reasoning Models to dynamically adjust their reasoning depth. This system reduces generated tokens by up to 43.9% while improving accuracy by up to 3.7 points on mathematical benchmarks, achieving a superior balance between efficiency and performance.

title: Stable Adaptive Thinking via Advantage Shaping and Length-Aware Gradient Regulation

abstract: Large language models (LLMs) empowered by chain-of-thought reasoning have achieved impressive accuracy on complex tasks but suffer from excessive inference costs and latency when applied uniformly to all problems. We propose SABER (Switchable and Balanced Training for Efficient LLM Reasoning), a reinforcement learning framework that endows LLMs with user-controllable, token-budgeted reasoning. SABER first profiles each training example's base-model thinking token usage and assigns it to one of the predefined budget tiers. During fine-tuning, the model is guided by system prompts and length-aware rewards to respect its assigned budget. In parallel, we incorporate no-think examples to ensure the model remains reliable even when explicit reasoning is turned off. SABER further supports four discrete inference modes - NoThink, FastThink, CoreThink, and DeepThink, enabling flexible trade-offs between latency and reasoning depth. Extensive evaluations on math reasoning (MATH, GSM8K), code generation (MBPP), and logical reasoning (LiveBench-Reasoning) demonstrate that SABER achieves high accuracy under tight budgets, graceful degradation, and effective cross-scale and cross-domain generalization. In particular, SABER-FastThink cuts reasoning length by 65.4% and yields a 3.6% accuracy gain compared with the base model on the MATH benchmark.
title: SABER: Switchable and Balanced Training for Efficient LLM Reasoning

abstract: Large reasoning models (LRMs) like OpenAI o1 and DeepSeek-R1 achieve high accuracy on complex tasks by adopting long chain-of-thought (CoT) reasoning paths. However, the inherent verbosity of these processes frequently results in redundancy and overthinking. To address this issue, existing works leverage Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) to reduce LRM output length, but their static length reward design cannot dynamically adapt according to the relative problem difficulty and response length distribution, causing over-compression and compromised accuracy. Therefore, we propose SmartThinker, a novel GRPO-based efficient reasoning method with progressive CoT length calibration. SmartThinker makes a two-fold contribution: First, it dynamically estimates the optimal length with peak accuracy during training and guides overlong responses toward it to reduce response length while sustaining accuracy. Second, it dynamically modulates the length reward coefficient to avoid the unwarranted penalization of correct reasoning paths. Extensive experiment results show that SmartThinker achieves up to 52.5% average length compression with improved accuracy, and achieves up to 16.6% accuracy improvement on challenging benchmarks like AIME25. The source code can be found at this https URL.

keyInsights: The optimal reasoning length is dynamic and problem-dependent, requiring adaptive mechanisms rather than static, heuristic length penalties used in prior reinforcement learning approaches.
Modeling the distributions of all generated reasoning lengths and only the correct reasoning lengths allows for a probabilistic derivation of a contextually optimal length to guide CoT generation.
Modulating the length reward coefficient is crucial to prevent penalizing correct yet longer reasoning paths that are often necessary for complex problem-solving, thereby safeguarding accuracy.
originalProblem: Large Reasoning Models (LRMs) often suffer from an "overthinking problem," where excessively long CoT outputs lead to redundancy, increased computational costs, and diminishing returns.
Existing efficient reasoning methods use static length rewards and fixed reward coefficients, which fail to adapt to problem difficulty or balance accuracy and length dynamically.
Reasoning accuracy does not monotonically improve with output length, necessitating an explicit guide towards an optimal length rather than indiscriminate compression or expansion.
results: SmartThinker achieved the highest combined accuracy and efficiency (AE Score) across benchmarks, improving reasoning accuracy by up to 16.6% (e.g., on AIME25) while reducing average token usage by up to 52.2%.
The method demonstrated difficulty-aware length adjustment, achieving higher compression for simpler problems and allowing longer outputs for challenging tasks, showcasing adaptive reasoning.

It exhibited strong generalizability across different model scales (1.5B, 4B, 7B parameters) and out-of-domain tasks (e.g., MMLU and code generation), maintaining or improving accuracy while compressing length.
solution: SmartThinker introduces a GRPO-based algorithm that dynamically estimates an optimal reasoning length by maximizing the conditional probability of a correct answer, modeled via Gaussian distributions of all and successful trajectory lengths.
It implements a dynamic length reward that penalizes only *correct* trajectories if their length exceeds the estimated optimal length, encouraging conciseness for efficient correct answers.

A dynamic length reward coefficient ensures that correct reasoning paths maintain a non-negative average advantage relative to their group, preventing accidental suppression of necessary longer reasoning.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers from Shanghai Jiao Tong University and Beihang University developed SmartThinker, a GRPO-based method that dynamically calibrates the length of Chain-of-Thought (CoT) reasoning in large language models. This approach reduces token usage by up to 52.2% while improving reasoning accuracy by up to 16.6% on math benchmarks, by adaptively adjusting CoT length to problem difficulty.

title: SmartThinker: Progressive Chain-of-Thought Length Calibration for Efficient Large Language Model Reasoning

abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) have demonstrated remarkable reasoning abilities on complex problems using long Chain-of-Thought (CoT) reasoning. However, they often suffer from overthinking, meaning generating unnecessarily lengthy reasoning steps for simpler problems. This issue may degrade the efficiency of the models and make them difficult to adapt the reasoning depth to the complexity of problems. To address this, we introduce a novel metric Token Entropy Cumulative Average (TECA), which measures the extent of exploration throughout the reasoning process. We further propose a novel reasoning paradigm named "Explore Briefly, Then Decide", with an associated Cumulative Entropy Regulation (CER) mechanism. This paradigm leverages TECA to help the model dynamically determine the optimal point to conclude its thought process and provide a final answer, thus achieving efficient reasoning. Experimental results across diverse mathematical benchmarks show that our approach substantially mitigates overthinking without sacrificing problem solving ability. With our thinking paradigm, the average response length decreases by up to 71% on simpler datasets, demonstrating the effectiveness of our method in creating a more efficient and adaptive reasoning process.
title: Explore Briefly, Then Decide: Mitigating LLM Overthinking via Cumulative Entropy Regulation

abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) often experience performance degradation during long-running interactions due to increasing context length, memory saturation, and computational overhead. This paper presents an adaptive context compression framework that integrates importance-aware memory selection, coherence-sensitive filtering, and dynamic budget allocation to retain essential conversational information while controlling context growth. The approach is evaluated on LOCOMO, LOCCO, and LongBench benchmarks to assess answer quality, retrieval accuracy, coherence preservation, and efficiency. Experimental results demonstrate that the proposed method achieves consistent improvements in conversational stability and retrieval performance while reducing token usage and inference latency compared with existing memory and compression-based approaches. These findings indicate that adaptive context compression provides an effective balance between long-term memory preservation and computational efficiency in persistent LLM interactions

keyInsights: Dynamically integrating semantic relevance, recency, and structural dependencies provides a more effective approach to prioritize context for retention than static pruning methods.
Formulating context management as a multi-objective optimization problem that includes task quality, coherence, and token reduction enables a practical balance between performance and efficiency.
Coherence-aware filtering is critical for preventing the loss of vital dialogue information during compression, thereby maintaining conversational stability and consistency in extended interactions.
originalProblem: Large Language Models (LLMs) experience performance degradation in long-running interactions due to the continuous growth of conversational context.
Context expansion leads to issues like memory saturation, high computational overhead, and 'context decay,' where earlier crucial information is lost.

Existing solutions for long-context processing often struggle with dynamic adaptation, maintaining conversational coherence, or balancing efficiency with information preservation under constrained context budgets.
results: The adaptive compression method demonstrated answer accuracy of 0.89–0.91 and coherence scores of 0.95–0.96, which matched or slightly exceeded existing memory-based approaches on long-running conversation benchmarks.
It achieved substantial efficiency gains, including a 25%–55% reduction in token usage and a 10%–35% improvement in inference latency.
The framework successfully maintained conversational consistency and long-term memory stability on LOCCO and LOCOMO datasets, with consistency in the range of 4.45–4.55 and coherence in 4.50–4.60.
solution: A novel adaptive context compression framework was developed, which models context management as a joint optimization problem balancing memory retention, coherence, and token efficiency.
The framework employs adaptive importance estimation, combining semantic relevance, recency, and structural dependencies to prioritize essential dialogue turns.

It dynamically adjusts the token budget based on interaction complexity (dialogue entropy) and uses a multi-objective optimization to balance task quality, coherence preservation, and token reduction.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers from LinkedIn and eBay introduced an adaptive context compression framework for Large Language Models, which dynamically manages conversational history to prevent context decay and improve efficiency in long-running interactions. The framework achieved answer accuracy of 0.89–0.91 and coherence scores of 0.95–0.96 while reducing token usage by 25%–55% and improving latency by 10%–35% across multiple benchmarks.

title: Developing Adaptive Context Compression Techniques for Large Language Models (LLMs) in Long-Running Interactions

abstract: While Long Chain-of-Thought (CoT) reasoning significantly improves Large Language Models (LLMs) performance on complex reasoning tasks, the substantial computational and memory costs of generating long CoT sequences limit their efficiency and practicality. Existing studies usually enhance the reasoning efficiency of LLMs by compressing CoT sequences. However, this approach conflicts with test-time scaling, limiting the reasoning capacity of LLMs. In this paper, we propose an efficient reasoning framework that models the reasoning process of LLMs as a state-transition process. Specifically, we first apply a linear attention mechanism to estimate the LLM's reasoning state, which records the historical reasoning information from previous reasoning steps. Then, based on the query prompt and the reasoning state, the LLM can efficiently perform the current reasoning step and update the state. With the linear attention, each token in the current reasoning step can directly retrieve relevant historical reasoning information from the reasoning state, without explicitly attending to tokens in previous reasoning steps. In this way, the computational complexity of attention is reduced from quadratic to linear, significantly improving the reasoning efficiency of LLMs. In addition, we propose a state-based reasoning strategy to mitigate the over-thinking issue caused by noisy reasoning steps. Extensive experiments across multiple datasets and model sizes demonstrate that our framework not only improves the reasoning efficiency of LLMs but also enhances their reasoning performance.

keyInsights: Modeling LLM reasoning as a state-transition process with specialized attention can resolve the efficiency-performance trade-off in CoT by decoupling reasoning depth from quadratic costs.

Linear attention can effectively maintain a compressed, global 'reasoning state' that captures historical information efficiently, offering constant memory and linear computational complexity for long contexts.
Dynamically guiding the reasoning trajectory using a global direction and promoting diverse thinking patterns significantly improves robustness against noisy reasoning steps and overall accuracy.
Knowledge distillation from a base LLM is crucial for successfully training the linear attention component to capture and utilize complex global reasoning information effectively.
originalProblem: Transformer-based LLMs incur prohibitive computational and memory costs for long Chain-of-Thought (CoT) reasoning due to quadratic attention complexity and linear KV-cache memory scaling.
Existing methods to improve LLM reasoning efficiency (e.g., CoT shortening, concise text representation, KV-cache reduction) often compromise reasoning ability or interpretability.
There is a fundamental conflict between the need for deep, multi-step reasoning in complex tasks and the practical limitations of current LLM architectures regarding efficiency and scalability.
results: The framework achieved significant efficiency improvements, demonstrating a 16-18% speedup on AIME24 and over 40% faster reasoning for extremely long (32K token) CoT sequences, while maintaining nearly constant memory usage.
It consistently outperformed the base model and other baselines, yielding an average performance gain of 4.7 points on mathematical reasoning benchmarks (e.g., GSM8K, MATH-500) for Qwen2.5-1.5B.

Ablation studies confirmed the critical contributions of the state-based reasoning strategy, diverse thinking patterns guidance, and the knowledge distillation loss, with the linear attention-based state mechanism being central to performance gains.

The model exhibited strong domain generalization, outperforming baselines on scientific (GPQA Diamond) and code (HumanEval) reasoning tasks, and is compatible with existing CoT-shortening methods for further improvements.
solution: The research proposes a state-transition framework that models LLM reasoning as a continuous state-transition process, addressing architectural inefficiencies directly.

It introduces a Mixed Attention Module (MAM) combining Softmax-Attention for local context within steps (clearing past KV-cache) and a Linear-Attention (LA) submodule to maintain a global compressed reasoning state matrix ($S_t$) with constant memory.

A state-based reasoning strategy is implemented, utilizing a dynamic correction mechanism from a 'global reasoning direction' and encouraging diverse thinking patterns to mitigate 'overthinking' and enhance robustness.
paper_summary.summary: A state-transition framework enables Large Language Models (LLMs) to perform Chain-of-Thought reasoning with significantly reduced computational and memory costs. This framework, developed by researchers from Xiamen University, Alibaba International Digital Commerce, and Shanghai AI Lab, offers up to a 40% speedup for long reasoning sequences and an average performance gain of 4.7 points on mathematical benchmarks, without compromising reasoning depth or interpretability.

title: A State-Transition Framework for Efficient LLM Reasoning

abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) increasingly rely on Chain-of-Thought (CoT) reasoning to improve accuracy on complex tasks. However, always generating lengthy reasoning traces is inefficient, leading to excessive token usage and higher inference costs. This paper introduces the Hybrid Policy Optimization (i.e., HiPO), a framework for adaptive reasoning control that enables LLMs to selectively decide when to engage in detailed reasoning (Think-on) and when to respond directly (Think-off). Specifically, HiPO combines a hybrid data pipelineproviding paired Think-on and Think-off responseswith a hybrid reinforcement learning reward system that balances accuracy and efficiency while avoiding over-reliance on detailed reasoning. Experiments across mathematics and coding benchmarks demonstrate that HiPO can substantially reduce token length while maintaining or improving accuracy. Finally, we hope HiPO a can be a principled approach for efficient adaptive reasoning, advancing the deployment of reasoning-oriented LLMs in real-world, resource-sensitive settings.

keyInsights: Explicitly biasing the hybrid training data towards the shortest correct response for a chosen reasoning mode is crucial for fostering efficiency in adaptive LLMs.

A dual advantage function design in the RL system, with separate signals for global mode selection and local response quality (with local normalization), effectively balances mode preference and intra-mode performance improvement.

The framework reveals that adaptive reasoning can significantly improve the practical deployment of LLMs by making complex reasoning more cost-effective and faster without sacrificing, and often enhancing, accuracy.
originalProblem: Large Language Models (LLMs) utilizing Chain-of-Thought (CoT) reasoning often 'overthink,' generating excessively long explanations even for simple queries.
This 'overthinking' leads to inflated token usage, increased inference latency, and higher computational costs, hindering efficient real-world deployment of LLMs.
Existing adaptive reasoning methods lack a principled way to balance accuracy and efficiency, often relying on coarse supervision or monotonic incentives that may sacrifice correctness for brevity.
results: HiPO achieved an average accuracy increase of 6.2% across benchmarks, while simultaneously reducing average token length by 30% and average thinking rate by 39% compared to the initial Cold-Start model.
On efficient tasks like HumanEval and MBPP, HiPO reduced token lengths by over 70% and 69% respectively, alongside improving accuracy, demonstrating strong efficiency gains.

Ablation studies confirmed that the 'shortest response' selection in data generation and the specific design of the dual advantage functions in RL are critical components for HiPO's balanced performance.
solution: HiPO introduces a two-stage training framework comprising a novel hybrid data construction pipeline and an advanced reinforcement learning (RL) reward system.

The hybrid data pipeline generates diverse training examples by sampling 'Think-on' (reasoning) and 'Think-off' (direct answer) responses, verifying correctness, and preferentially selecting the shortest correct response for the optimal mode.

The RL system employs a hybrid reward formulation with distinct 'Judge' (mode selection) and 'Answer' (response quality) advantage functions, incorporating bias adjustment and local normalization to optimize token-level generation for both efficiency and accuracy.
paper_summary.summary: The HiPO framework enables Large Language Models (LLMs) to dynamically adjust their reasoning depth, deciding when to provide direct answers or engage in step-by-step thinking. This approach significantly reduces token usage and latency while simultaneously improving accuracy across various complex mathematics and coding tasks.

title: HiPO: Hybrid Policy Optimization for Dynamic Reasoning in LLMs

abstract: Recent advances in Large Reasoning Models (LRMs) have demonstrated strong performance on complex tasks through long Chain-of-Thought (CoT) reasoning. However, their lengthy outputs increase computational costs and may lead to overthinking, raising challenges in balancing reasoning effectiveness and efficiency. Current methods for efficient reasoning often compromise reasoning quality or require extensive resources. This paper investigates efficient methods to reduce the generation length of LRMs. We analyze generation path distributions and filter generated trajectories through difficulty estimation. Subsequently, we analyze the convergence behaviors of the objectives of various preference optimization methods under a Bradley-Terry loss based framework. Based on the analysis, we propose Length Controlled Preference Optimization (LCPO) that directly balances the implicit reward related to NLL loss. LCPO can effectively learn length preference with limited data and training. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our approach significantly reduces the average output length by over 50\% across multiple benchmarks while maintaining the reasoning performance. Our work highlights the potential for computationally efficient approaches in guiding LRMs toward efficient reasoning.

organization_info.name: University of Science and Technology of China

title: Pruning Long Chain-of-Thought of Large Reasoning Models via Small-Scale Preference Optimization

abstract: Large Reasoning Models (LRMs) have demonstrated impressive capabilities but suffer from cognitive inefficiencies like "overthinking" simple problems and "underthinking" complex ones. While existing methods that use supervised fine-tuning (SFT) or reinforcement learning (RL) with token-length rewards can improve efficiency, they often do so at the cost of accuracy. This paper introduces DeepCompress, a novel framework that simultaneously enhances both the accuracy and efficiency of LRMs. We challenge the prevailing approach of consistently favoring shorter reasoning paths, showing that longer responses can contain a broader range of correct solutions for difficult problems. DeepCompress employs an adaptive length reward mechanism that dynamically classifies problems as "Simple" or "Hard" in real-time based on the model's evolving capability. It encourages shorter, more efficient reasoning for "Simple" problems while promoting longer, more exploratory thought chains for "Hard" problems. This dual-reward strategy enables the model to autonomously adjust its Chain-of-Thought (CoT) length, compressing reasoning for well-mastered problems and extending it for those it finds challenging. Experimental results on challenging mathematical benchmarks show that DeepCompress consistently outperforms baseline methods, achieving superior accuracy while significantly improving token efficiency.
title: DeepCompress: A Dual Reward Strategy for Dynamically Exploring and Compressing Reasoning Chains

abstract:
While reasoning-augmented large language models (RLLMs) significantly enhance
complex task performance through extended reasoning chains, they inevitably
introduce substantial unnecessary token consumption, particularly for simpler
problems where Short Chain-of-Thought (Short CoT) suffices. This overthinking
phenomenon leads to inefficient resource usage without proportional accuracy
gains. To address this issue, we propose Self-Route, a dynamic reasoning
framework that automatically selects between general and reasoning modes based
on model capability estimation. Our approach introduces a lightweight
pre-inference stage to extract capability-aware embeddings from hidden layer
representations, enabling real-time evaluation of the model's ability to solve
problems. We further construct Gradient-10K, a model difficulty
estimation-based dataset with dense complexity sampling, to train the router
for precise capability boundary detection. Extensive experiments demonstrate
that Self-Route achieves comparable accuracy to reasoning models while reducing
token consumption by 30-55\% across diverse benchmarks. The proposed framework
demonstrates consistent effectiveness across models with different parameter
scales and reasoning paradigms, highlighting its general applicability and
practical value.

keyInsights: The Gradient-10K dataset, which features a dense spectrum of problem complexities and quantifies difficulty based on a model's accuracy, is crucial for training a precise capability detection router.

Internal model representations from intermediate-to-higher transformer layers (around 60-80% position) provide effective 'capability-aware embeddings' for estimating a model's solving probability with minimal computational overhead.

A lightweight linear router, trained with binary cross-entropy loss on these capability embeddings, can accurately and efficiently route queries based on estimated problem difficulty and model capability.
originalProblem: Reasoning-augmented Large Language Models (RLLMs) often apply extensive Long Chain-of-Thought (CoT) reasoning to simple problems, leading to 'overthinking'.
This 'overthinking' results in significant token waste, increased inference latency, and higher computational costs for deploying RLLMs.
Existing efficiency solutions often compromise accuracy or lack a nuanced understanding of when complex reasoning is truly necessary.
results: Self-Route achieved a 30-55% reduction in token consumption across evaluated models and datasets while maintaining high accuracy, with less than 2% accuracy loss compared to pure reasoning models.

The framework demonstrated adaptive behavior, predominantly using efficient Short CoT for simple tasks (e.g., GSM8K) and appropriately selecting Long CoT for complex tasks (e.g., GPQA Diamond, AIME-2024).

Routers trained on the Gradient-10K dataset consistently outperformed those trained on uniform datasets, showing up to an 11% improvement in routing accuracy due to the fine-grained difficulty gradients.
solution: Self-Route introduces a dynamic routing mechanism that leverages a model's internal representations to decide reasoning depth.

The framework utilizes a lightweight Router to estimate the probability a general model can solve a problem, routing queries to a General Model (for Short CoT) or a Reasoning Model (for Long CoT) based on a confidence threshold.
A pre-inference stage with a constrained token budget extracts 'capability-aware embeddings' from the general model's hidden states, which the router then uses to make routing decisions.
paper_summary.summary: Self-Route is a framework enabling reasoning-augmented Large Language Models to dynamically switch between efficient Short CoT and thorough Long CoT reasoning based on an internal assessment of problem difficulty. Developed by Yang He and colleagues at Harbin Institute of Technology, it achieved 30-55% token reduction with less than 2% accuracy loss across various reasoning benchmarks, addressing the issue of 'overthinking' in LLMs.

title: Self-Route: Automatic Mode Switching via Capability Estimation for  Efficient Reasoning

abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) have shown impressive capabilities in complex reasoning tasks. However, current approaches employ uniform language density for both intermediate reasoning and final answers, leading to computational inefficiency. Our observation found that reasoning process serves a computational function for the model itself, while answering serves a communicative function for human understanding. This distinction enables the use of compressed, symbol-rich language for intermediate computations while maintaining human-readable final explanations. To address this inefficiency, we present Denser: \underline{D}ual-d\underline{ens}ity inf\underline{er}ence, a novel framework that optimizes information density separately for reasoning and answering phases. Our framework implements this through three components: a query processing module that analyzes input problems, a high-density compressed reasoning mechanism for efficient intermediate computations, and an answer generation component that translates compressed reasoning into human-readable solutions. Experimental evaluation across multiple reasoning question answering benchmarks demonstrates that Denser reduces token consumption by up to 62\% compared to standard Chain-of-Thought methods while preserving or improving accuracy. These efficiency gains are particularly significant for complex multi-step reasoning problems where traditional methods generate extensive explanations.

keyInsights: Empirical analysis shows that language used for computational reasoning phases is 4.3 times more information-dense than explanatory phases, yet explanatory language consumes approximately 68% of total tokens.
There exists a fundamental 'representational mismatch' where LLMs wrap a computational core in verbose natural language, leading to significant inefficiency for structured reasoning tasks.

Aligning information density with the functional requirement of each processing stage (high-density for internal computation, lower-density for external communication) can yield substantial efficiency and accuracy gains.
originalProblem: Large Language Models (LLMs) uniformly employ verbose natural language for both internal reasoning steps and external answers, leading to computational inefficiency.
This undifferentiated use of language, while good for human communication, is suboptimal for the high information density and strict structures required by complex reasoning tasks.
Existing Test-Time Scaling (TTS) methods enhance reasoning accuracy but exacerbate token consumption and computational costs due to verbose internal explanations.
results: Denser achieved an average accuracy improvement of 1.8% over the strongest baseline (Self-Verification) across math, logic, code, and general QA benchmarks, with pronounced gains in math and logic.
The framework reduced token consumption by up to 62% compared to standard Chain-of-Thought methods and decreased computational latency by 27.8% on the GSM8K dataset.

Performance benefits and efficiency improvements were consistent across various LLM sizes (0.5B to 57B-A14B) and became more pronounced with increasing problem complexity, demonstrating broad applicability and robustness.
solution: The Denser framework introduces a dual-density inference approach, utilizing compressed, symbolic representations for internal computations and human-readable language for final outputs.

It employs a Query Analysis Module to classify problem domains, a High-density Reasoning Module for efficient intermediate deductions using compressed language, and a Low-density Answering Module to translate compressed reasoning into user-friendly explanations.

The approach uses sophisticated prompt engineering and domain-specific compression operators to dynamically adjust language density, optimizing token count while preserving essential computational information.
paper_summary.summary: A dual-density inference framework named Denser optimizes Large Language Model (LLM) reasoning by decoupling internal computational language from external communicative language. This framework reduces token consumption by up to 62% and achieves 27.8% faster inference, while improving reasoning accuracy by an average of 1.8% across diverse benchmarks like math, logic, and coding.

title: Dual-Density Inference for Efficient Language Model Reasoning

abstract: Large language models (LLMs) have demonstrated remarkable capabilities in tasks requiring reasoning and multi-step problem-solving through the use of chain-of-thought (CoT) prompting. However, generating the full CoT process results in significantly longer output sequences, leading to increased computational costs and latency during inference. To address this challenge, we propose a novel approach to compress the CoT process through semantic alignment, enabling more efficient decoding while preserving the benefits of CoT reasoning. Our method introduces an auxiliary CoT model that learns to generate and compress the full thought process into a compact special token representation semantically aligned with the original CoT output. This compressed representation is then integrated into the input of the Hidden Chain-of-Thought (HCoT) model. The training process follows a two-stage procedure: First, the CoT model is optimized to generate the compressed token representations aligned with the ground-truth CoT outputs using a contrastive loss. Subsequently, with the CoT model parameters frozen, the HCoT model is fine-tuned to generate accurate subsequent predictions conditioned on the prefix instruction and the compressed CoT representations from the CoT model. Extensive experiments across three challenging domains - mathematical reasoning, agent invocation, and question answering - demonstrate that our semantic compression approach achieves competitive or improved performance compared to the full CoT baseline, while providing significant speedups of at least 1.5x in decoding time. Moreover, incorporating contrastive learning objectives further enhances the quality of the compressed representations, leading to better CoT prompting and improved task accuracy. Our work paves the way for more efficient exploitation of multi-step reasoning capabilities in LLMs across a wide range of applications.

keyInsights: Complex multi-step reasoning processes in LLMs can be effectively compressed into a single specialized token representation without significant loss of accuracy.
A two-stage disentangled fine-tuning paradigm, involving an auxiliary model for CoT compression and a main HCoT model for prediction, enables efficient and accurate reasoning.
Incorporating a contrastive learning objective during training enhances the semantic quality of the compressed reasoning token, leading to improved task performance.
originalProblem: Traditional Chain-of-Thought (CoT) prompting, while improving reasoning, leads to computational inefficiency due to generating lengthy intermediate steps.
The extended generation process of CoT increases inference latency, limiting its applicability in real-time systems and interactive applications.
Higher computational and memory requirements of CoT pose challenges for deploying reasoning-capable LLMs in resource-constrained environments.
results: The HCoT model achieved performance comparable to or better than standard full CoT baselines across challenging reasoning datasets like GSM8K, MATH, ScienceQA, and HotpotQA.
HCoT demonstrated significant speedups in decoding time, ranging from 1.5x to 3.8x compared to traditional CoT methods.
The integration of contrastive learning further improved the quality of compressed reasoning representations, leading to enhanced task accuracy.
solution: Introduces the Hidden Chain-of-Thought (HCoT) framework, which compresses the LLM's multi-step reasoning into a single specialized "[CoT]" token.

Employs a novel two-stage fine-tuning process: first, an auxiliary model learns to compress reasoning into the token, then the HCoT model is fine-tuned to predict based on this compressed representation.
Utilizes a contrastive loss function to ensure the compressed "[CoT]" token's embedding is semantically aligned with the full, explicit reasoning chain.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers from TAL Education Group and Jinan University developed Hidden Chain-of-Thought (HCoT), a framework that compresses LLM reasoning into a single specialized token. This approach yields 1.5x to 3.8x speedups in decoding time while maintaining or improving reasoning accuracy on complex tasks such as grade school math, competition-level mathematics, science questions, and multi-hop question answering.

title: Expediting and Elevating Large Language Model Reasoning via Hidden Chain-of-Thought Decoding

abstract: Reasoning-oriented Large Language Models (LLMs) often rely on generating explicit tokens step by step, and their effectiveness typically hinges on large-scale supervised fine-tuning or reinforcement learning. While Chain-of-Thought (CoT) techniques substantially enhance performance on complex reasoning tasks, they remain inefficient, requiring long reasoning traces that increase latency and token usage. In this work, we introduce Latent Codebooks for Fast Thinking, a framework that uses concise CoT sketches only during training to learn a codebook of discrete strategy priors. At inference, the model conditions on a handful of continuous thinking vectors distilled from the codebook in a single pass, enabling strategy-level guidance without producing explicit reasoning tokens. To complement this design, we propose GainRouter, a lightweight routing mechanism that adaptively switches between fast codebook guided inference and slow explicit reasoning, thereby suppressing overthinking and reducing unnecessary token generation. Experiments across multiple reasoning benchmarks show that our approach achieves competitive or superior accuracy while substantially lowering inference cost, offering a practical path toward efficient and controllable reasoning in large language models.

keyInsights: Explicit, lengthy reasoning traces can be substituted by compact, continuous "thinking vectors" derived from a latent codebook, significantly enhancing inference efficiency without sacrificing accuracy.

A two-stage training process, involving alignment of latent representations with explicit rationales followed by fine-tuning for task performance, effectively imbues LLMs with fast-thinking capabilities.

Dynamic routing based on problem complexity is crucial for balancing efficiency and accuracy, allowing LLMs to adaptively choose between fast, internalized reasoning and more resource-intensive slow thinking.
originalProblem: Large Language Models (LLMs) employing Chain-of-Thought (CoT) for complex reasoning incur substantial inference costs and latency due to extensive token generation.
LLMs often "overthink" on simpler problems, generating unnecessary reasoning steps that waste resources and can introduce errors.
Training advanced multi-step reasoning capabilities in LLMs typically requires complex and resource-intensive paradigms like process supervision or reinforcement learning.
results: The framework significantly reduced average generation length (e.g., from 19411 to 12797 tokens on AIME, and 3573 to 1348 tokens on MBPP) compared to full Chain-of-Thought methods.

It achieved competitive or superior accuracy on mathematical reasoning (e.g., 73.3% on AIME) and programming benchmarks (e.g., 92.55% Pass@1 on HumanEval), matching or slightly surpassing full CoT performance.
The approach effectively transferred reasoning capabilities to smaller models, improving Qwen2-1.5B's Pass@1 on MBPP from 28.06% to 35.49% and on HumanEval from 37.89% to 42.86%.
solution: The Latent Codebooks for Fast Thinking (LC-FT) mechanism internalizes reasoning strategies into a compact codebook of discrete priors, learned from concise CoT sketches during training.
At inference time, LC-FT conditions the LLM's decoding with a small number of continuous "thinking vectors" derived from the codebook in a single pass, bypassing explicit rationale generation.

GainRouter, an adaptive routing mechanism, intelligently switches between the efficient codebook-guided inference and traditional Chain-of-Thought reasoning only for problems requiring deeper processing, thereby preventing overthinking.
paper_summary.summary: A framework called "Fast Thinking for Large Language Models" (LC-FT) was developed, integrating a latent codebook of reasoning strategies and an adaptive router. This approach enables large language models to achieve competitive reasoning accuracy with significantly reduced inference latency and token usage compared to traditional Chain-of-Thought methods.

title: Fast Thinking for Large Language Models

abstract: External memory systems are pivotal for enabling Large Language Model (LLM) agents to maintain persistent knowledge and perform long-horizon decision-making. Existing paradigms typically follow a two-stage process: computationally expensive memory construction (e.g., structuring data into graphs) followed by naive retrieval-augmented generation. However, our empirical analysis reveals two fundamental limitations: complex construction incurs high costs with marginal performance gains, and simple context concatenation fails to bridge the gap between retrieval recall and reasoning accuracy. To address these challenges, we propose CoM (Chain-of-Memory), a novel framework that advocates for a paradigm shift toward lightweight construction paired with sophisticated utilization. CoM introduces a Chain-of-Memory mechanism that organizes retrieved fragments into coherent inference paths through dynamic evolution, utilizing adaptive truncation to prune irrelevant noise. Extensive experiments on the LongMemEval and LoCoMo benchmarks demonstrate that CoM outperforms strong baselines with accuracy gains of 7.5%-10.4%, while drastically reducing computational overhead to approximately 2.7% of token consumption and 6.0% of latency compared to complex memory architectures.

keyInsights: Complex, pre-structured memory construction is often inefficient; lightweight construction paired with sophisticated utilization is more effective and resource-friendly.
Dynamically organizing retrieved fragments into coherent, evolving inference paths (memory chains) overcomes the 'reasoning bottleneck' of isolated context concatenation in RAG.
Adaptive pruning of memory chains is crucial for maintaining context quality, preventing noise, and ensuring focused reasoning without explicit length limits.
originalProblem: Existing LLM agent memory systems incur high computational and temporal costs for complex structured memory construction without yielding proportional performance gains.
Naive Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) approaches suffer from a 'reasoning bottleneck,' where even relevant retrieved information often fails to translate into accurate, complex reasoning.
LLM agents struggle with long-term knowledge accumulation and coherent long-horizon decision-making due to limitations of context windows and inefficient memory management.
results: CoM achieved 76.40% accuracy on LongMemEval and 70.97% on LoCoMo with a Qwen3-32B backbone, representing gains of up to 15.8% over strong baselines, especially in multi-hop and temporal reasoning tasks.
The framework drastically reduced computational costs, requiring approximately 2.7% of token consumption and 6.0% of the time compared to complex memory architectures like A-Mem.

Ablation studies confirmed that both Dynamic Memory Chain Evolution and Adaptive Path Truncation are critical for CoM's performance and efficiency, with their removal leading to significant accuracy drops and increased costs.
solution: Proposes a lightweight, flat memory construction where raw interaction history is stored as simple memory nodes to minimize pre-processing overhead.

Introduces Dynamic Memory Chain Evolution (DMCE) which iteratively expands memory chains from retrieved candidates based on a multiplicative gating score combining query relevance and contextual consistency.
Implements Adaptive Path Truncation (APT) to dynamically terminate chain expansion when semantic relevance drops, preventing semantic drift and information redundancy.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers from the Chinese Academy of Sciences developed Chain-of-Memory (CoM), a framework for LLM agents that utilizes lightweight memory construction and dynamic memory chain evolution to significantly improve reasoning accuracy while drastically reducing computational overhead. CoM achieved up to 15.8% higher accuracy on complex reasoning tasks compared to baselines, with only 2.7% of the token consumption and 6.0% of the runtime of complex memory systems.

title: Chain-of-Memory: Lightweight Memory Construction with Dynamic Evolution for LLM Agents

abstract: Large language model (LLM) agents face fundamental limitations in long-horizon reasoning due to finite context windows, making effective memory management critical. Existing methods typically handle long-term memory (LTM) and short-term memory (STM) as separate components, relying on heuristics or auxiliary controllers, which limits adaptability and end-to-end optimization. In this paper, we propose Agentic Memory (AgeMem), a unified framework that integrates LTM and STM management directly into the agent's policy. AgeMem exposes memory operations as tool-based actions, enabling the LLM agent to autonomously decide what and when to store, retrieve, update, summarize, or discard information. To train such unified behaviors, we propose a three-stage progressive reinforcement learning strategy and design a step-wise GRPO to address sparse and discontinuous rewards induced by memory operations. Experiments on five long-horizon benchmarks demonstrate that AgeMem consistently outperforms strong memory-augmented baselines across multiple LLM backbones, achieving improved task performance, higher-quality long-term memory, and more efficient context usage.

keyInsights: Treating LTM and STM operations as intrinsic, learnable tool-based actions within an LLM's policy enables autonomous and adaptive memory management for complex tasks.

A progressive three-stage reinforcement learning strategy combined with step-wise GRPO successfully facilitates the learning of sophisticated, long-horizon memory behaviors despite sparse and discontinuous reward signals.

Optimizing memory management requires a composite reward function that explicitly evaluates both task completion and the quality of memory operations, rather than relying solely on final task performance.
originalProblem: Large language models (LLMs) face finite context window limitations, hindering their ability to retain and process information over long interactions and complex tasks.
Existing memory management approaches for LLM agents treat long-term (LTM) and short-term (STM) memory as distinct, loosely coupled modules, leading to fragmented control and suboptimal performance.

Training unified memory policies for LLM agents is challenging due to the mismatch of standard reinforcement learning assumptions with the sparse and discontinuous rewards inherent in memory operations.
results: AgeMem consistently achieved the highest average performance across five long-horizon benchmarks, improving task completion by 4.82 to 8.57 percentage points over leading baselines.

The framework generated higher-quality long-term memories (e.g., MQ of 0.605 on HotpotQA for Qwen3-4B) and demonstrated more efficient short-term context usage, reducing prompt token counts by up to 5.1%.
Ablation studies confirmed that the progressive RL training and the multi-component reward function were crucial for learning effective, adaptive memory management and coordinating LTM and STM tools.
solution: Introduced Agentic Memory (AgeMem), a unified framework that integrates LTM and STM management by exposing memory operations as learnable, tool-based actions within the LLM agent's policy.
Developed a three-stage progressive reinforcement learning strategy for end-to-end training of the agent's memory policy, addressing challenges of sparse and discontinuous rewards.

Utilized a step-wise Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) to assign credit consistently across long, multi-stage trajectories and a composite reward function for comprehensive evaluation of memory and task performance.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers from Alibaba Group and Wuhan University developed Agentic Memory (AgeMem), a framework enabling large language model agents to autonomously manage both long-term and short-term memory through learnable tool-based actions. This approach, trained with a three-stage progressive reinforcement learning strategy, enhances task completion rates, improves the quality of stored knowledge, and optimizes context utilization across various long-horizon reasoning benchmarks.

title: Agentic Memory: Learning Unified Long-Term and Short-Term Memory Management for Large Language Model Agents

abstract: Chain-of-Thought (CoT) reasoning enhances the problem-solving ability of large language models (LLMs) but leads to substantial inference overhead, limiting deployment in resource-constrained settings. This paper investigates efficient CoT transfer across models of different scales and architectures through an adaptive reasoning summarization framework. The proposed method compresses reasoning traces via semantic segmentation with importance scoring, budget-aware dynamic compression, and coherence reconstruction, preserving critical reasoning steps while significantly reducing token usage. Experiments on 7{,}501 medical examination questions across 10 specialties show up to 40% higher accuracy than truncation under the same token budgets. Evaluations on 64 model pairs from eight LLMs (1.5B-32B parameters, including DeepSeek-R1 and Qwen3) confirm strong cross-model transferability. Furthermore, a Gaussian Process-based Bayesian optimization module reduces evaluation cost by 84% and reveals a power-law relationship between model size and cross-domain robustness. These results demonstrate that reasoning summarization provides a practical path toward efficient CoT transfer, enabling advanced reasoning under tight computational constraints. Code will be released upon publication.
title: CoT-X: An Adaptive Framework for Cross-Model Chain-of-Thought Transfer and Optimization

abstract: Large Reasoning Language Models (LRLMs or LRMs) demonstrate remarkable capabilities in complex reasoning tasks, but suffer from significant computational inefficiencies due to overthinking phenomena. Existing efficient reasoning methods face the challenge of balancing reasoning quality with inference cost reduction. We propose \textbf{Adaptive Reasoning Suppression (ARS)}, a novel training-free approach that dynamically suppresses redundant reasoning steps while preserving accuracy through adaptive certainty monitoring. ARS introduces a multi-checkpoint certainty estimation mechanism with progressive suppression thresholds, achieving superior efficiency compared to static suppression methods. Our extensive evaluation across mathematical reasoning benchmarks using multiple model architectures demonstrates that ARS achieves up to 53%, 46.1%, and 57.9% in token, latency and energy reduction, while maintaining or improving accuracy.
title: ARS: Adaptive Reasoning Suppression for Efficient Large Reasoning Language Models

abstract: Large language model (LLM) agents face fundamental limitations in long-horizon reasoning due to finite context windows, making effective memory management critical. Existing methods typically handle long-term memory (LTM) and short-term memory (STM) as separate components, relying on heuristics or auxiliary controllers, which limits adaptability and end-to-end optimization. In this paper, we propose Agentic Memory (AgeMem), a unified framework that integrates LTM and STM management directly into the agent's policy. Experiments on five long-horizon benchmarks demonstrate that AgeMem consistently outperforms strong memory-augmented baselines across multiple LLM backbones, achieving improved task performance, higher-quality long-term memory, and more efficient context usage.
title: Agentic Memory: Learning Unified Long-Term and Short-Term Memory Management for Large Language Model Agents

abstract: Language Reasoning Models (LRMs) achieve strong performance by scaling test-time computation but often suffer from ``overthinking'', producing excessively long reasoning traces that increase latency and memory usage. Existing LRMs typically enforce conciseness with uniform length penalties, which over-compress crucial early deduction steps at the sequence level and indiscriminately penalize all queries at the group level. To solve these limitations, we propose \textbf{\model}, a dual-level framework for prefix-protected and difficulty-aware compression under hierarchical supervision. At the sequence level, prefix-protected optimization employs decaying mixed rollouts to maintain valid reasoning paths while promoting conciseness. At the group level, difficulty-aware penalty dynamically scales length constraints based on query complexity, maintaining exploration for harder questions while curbing redundancy on easier ones. Extensive experiments on DeepSeek-R1-Distill-Qwen (1.5B/7B) demonstrate that \model achieves a substantial reduction in token usage (up to \textbf{55.7\%}) while simultaneously improving accuracy (up to \textbf{4.1\%}) on math benchmarks, with generalization ability to code, science, and general domains.

keyInsights: Achieving significant token reduction in LRMs is possible without compromising, and even improving, reasoning accuracy by employing adaptive, dual-level compression.
Protecting early reasoning steps (prefix-protection) and adapting compression based on query difficulty are crucial for both performance and training stability in efficient reasoning.
Efficient reasoning stems from reducing redundant reflection and verification steps rather than truncating essential logical advancements, leading to more decisive problem-solving behavior.
originalProblem: Language Reasoning Models (LRMs) suffer from "overthinking," generating excessively long and redundant reasoning traces, leading to high computational overhead (latency, memory).
Existing compression methods for LRMs often face a trade-off, either sacrificing reasoning accuracy for efficiency or achieving limited efficiency gains.

Current length penalties are often indiscriminate, over-compressing crucial early deduction steps at the sequence level and failing to adapt to the varying difficulty of different queries at the group level.
results: PACE achieved an average token reduction rate of 55.7% on mathematical benchmarks for a 7B model, while simultaneously improving average accuracy by 0.6% over vanilla baselines.
The framework demonstrated strong generalization, improving both accuracy (up to 4.6%) and token efficiency (up to 37.0%) on out-of-domain tasks like science, code, and instruction following.

Ablation studies confirmed that both prefix-protected optimization and difficulty-aware penalties are essential, with their absence leading to significant performance degradation and training instability.
solution: PACE introduces a dual-level framework within a hierarchical reinforcement learning setup, modulating the reward signal to apply adaptive compression.
Prefix-Protected Optimization safeguards initial, critical reasoning steps by using a rejection-sampled "prefix policy" with a decaying length, ensuring foundational logical coherence is maintained.

Difficulty-Aware Penalty dynamically scales the length penalty based on the empirical pass rate of multiple sampled outputs for a query, relaxing penalties for complex tasks and enforcing stricter ones for simpler tasks.
paper_summary.summary: Researchers from UESTC, Nanbeige Lab at BOSS Zhipin, and Peking University developed PACE, a dual-level framework that reduces reasoning overthinking in Language Reasoning Models. PACE achieves an average token reduction of 55.7% while simultaneously improving reasoning accuracy by 0.6% on mathematical benchmarks for the 7B model, resolving a common efficiency-accuracy trade-off.

title: PACE: Prefix-Protected and Difficulty-Aware Compression for Efficient Reasoning

title: Fast Thinking for Large Language Models

title: Agentic Memory: Learning Unified Long-Term and Short-Term Memory Management for Large Language Model Agents

abstract: Large reasoning models (LRMs) achieve strong accuracy through test-time scaling, generating longer chains of thought or sampling multiple solutions, but at steep costs in tokens and latency. We argue that memory is a core ingredient for efficient reasoning: when evidence already exists, models should think less by reusing structured memory instead of recomputing derivations. We present ENGRAM-R, an inference-time memory layer that integrates typed retrieval with compact fact card representations and explicit citation control. On the LoCoMo benchmark, ENGRAM-R reduces input tokens by 85% and reasoning tokens by 75% compared to full context while maintaining high accuracy. On a multi-hop slice of the LongMemEval benchmark, it achieves similar efficiency with substantial accuracy gains. These results show that memory is not only critical for long-horizon correctness but also a practical lever for efficient reasoning under tight compute, memory, and latency budgets.
title: Reuse, Don't Recompute: Efficient Large Reasoning Model Inference via Memory Orchestration

abstract: Being prompted to engage in reasoning has emerged as a core technique for using large language models (LLMs), deploying additional inference-time compute to improve task performance. However, as LLMs increase in both size and adoption, inference costs are correspondingly becoming increasingly burdensome. How, then, might we optimize reasoning's cost-performance tradeoff? This work introduces a novel approach based on computational models of metareasoning used in cognitive science, training LLMs to selectively use intermediate reasoning steps only when necessary. We first develop a reward function that incorporates the Value of Computation by penalizing unnecessary reasoning, then use this reward function with Expert Iteration to train the LLM. Compared to few-shot chain-of-thought prompting and STaR, our method significantly reduces inference costs (20-37\% fewer tokens generated across three models) while maintaining task performance across diverse datasets.
title: Rational Metareasoning for Large Language Models

abstract: To support reliable long-term interaction in complex environments, LLM agents require memory systems that efficiently manage historical experiences. Existing approaches either retain full interaction histories via passive context extension, leading to substantial redundancy, or rely on iterative reasoning to filter noise, incurring high token costs. To address this challenge, we introduce SimpleMem, an efficient memory framework based on semantic lossless compression. We propose a three-stage pipeline designed to maximize information density and token utilization: (1) \textit{Semantic Structured Compression}, which applies entropy-aware filtering to distill unstructured interactions into compact, multi-view indexed memory units; (2) \textit{Recursive Memory Consolidation}, an asynchronous process that integrates related units into higher-level abstract representations to reduce redundancy; and (3) \textit{Adaptive Query-Aware Retrieval}, which dynamically adjusts retrieval scope based on query complexity to construct precise context efficiently. Experiments on benchmark datasets show that our method consistently outperforms baseline approaches in accuracy, retrieval efficiency, and inference cost, achieving an average F1 improvement of 26.4% while reducing inference-time token consumption by up to 30-fold, demonstrating a superior balance between performance and efficiency. Code is available at this https URL.
title: SimpleMem: Efficient Lifelong Memory for LLM Agents

Experiments on five long-horizon benchmarks demonstrate that AgeMem consistently outperforms strong memory-augmented baselines across multiple LLM backbones, achieving improved task performance, higher-quality long-term memory, and more efficient context usage.

abstract: To support reliable long-term interaction in complex environments, LLM agents require memory systems that efficiently manage historical experiences. Existing approaches either retain full interaction histories via passive context extension, leading to substantial redundancy, or rely on iterative reasoning to filter noise, incurring high token costs. To address this challenge, we introduce SimpleMem, an efficient memory framework based on semantic lossless compression. We propose a three-stage pipeline designed to maximize information density and token utilization: (1) \textit{Semantic Structured Compression}, which applies entropy-aware filtering to distill unstructured interactions into compact, multi-view indexed memory units; (2) \textit{Recursive Memory Consolidation}, an asynchronous process that integrates related units into higher-level abstract representations to reduce redundancy; and (3) \textit{Adaptive Query-Aware Retrieval}, which dynamically adjusts retrieval scope based on query complexity to construct precise context efficiently. Experiments on benchmark datasets show that our method consistently outperforms baseline approaches in accuracy, retrieval efficiency, and inference cost, achieving an average F1 improvement of 26.4% while reducing inference-time token consumption by up to 30-fold, demonstrating a superior balance between performance and efficiency. Code is available at this https URL.
title: SimpleMem: Efficient Lifelong Memory for LLM Agents

abstract: Reasoning-oriented Large Language Models (LLMs) often rely on generating explicit tokens step by step, and their effectiveness typically hinges on large-scale supervised fine-tuning or reinforcement learning. While Chain-of-Thought (CoT) techniques substantially enhance performance on complex reasoning tasks, they remain inefficient, requiring long reasoning traces that increase latency and token usage. In this work, we introduce Latent Codebooks for Fast Thinking, a framework that uses concise CoT sketches only during training to learn a codebook of discrete strategy priors. At inference, the model conditions on a handful of continuous thinking vectors distilled from the codebook in a single pass, enabling strategy-level guidance without producing explicit reasoning tokens. To complement this design, we propose GainRouter, a lightweight routing mechanism that adaptively switches between fast codebook guided inference and slow explicit reasoning, thereby suppressing overthinking and reducing unnecessary token generation. Experiments across multiple reasoning benchmarks show that our approach achieves competitive or superior accuracy while substantially lowering inference cost, offering a practical path toward efficient and controllable reasoning in large language models.
title: Fast Thinking for Large Language Models
